steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage opens by saying Smith is "famed for" the invisible hand metaphor, then immediately undercuts this with "putatively." The key facts: Smith used the metaphor only once, made a narrow point, it was largely ignored, and later economists eager to secure an intellectual pedigree elevated it. The main idea is about how Smith's reputation for this metaphor was built by others, not earned by the metaphor's actual role in his work.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Some twentieth-century economists gave Smith's metaphor... a significance it does not have... but it is nevertheless a useful model...': The passage explicitly frames "useful model" as the putative claim, then disputes it. Adding "nevertheless" endorses the metaphor — the passage does not. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Smith's metaphor... was intended as a subtle critique of mercantilism': The passage says Smith used it to make a narrow point about mercantilism, but does not say it was a "subtle critique." This mischaracterizes Smith's intent. ✗
  • Choice C — 'The reputation of Smith's metaphor... is not due to the importance of the metaphor in Smith's work but rather to the promotion of the metaphor by some later economists for their own ends.': This matches the passage exactly — ignored in Smith's time, elevated later by economists seeking intellectual legitimacy. ✓
  • Choice D — '...economists came to realize that the metaphor was a robust model that anticipated their own views.': The passage says economists elevated it to secure a pedigree for views they already had — not because they discovered it was genuinely robust. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
